sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Beta blockers, also known as β-blockers, are a class of medications primarily used to manage heart and circulatory system conditions. They work by blocking beta-adrenergic receptors, which reduce the effects of stress hormones like adrenaline and noradrenaline on the heart and blood vessels. Toxicity from accidental or intentional overdose is not uncommon, causing bradycardia that is associated with significant mortality. Commonly used beta blockers include Bisoprolol, Atenolol, Metoprolol, Propranolol and Sotalol.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Cardiovascular effects:**
  - Bradycardia
  - Heart block, sinus node dysfunction
  - Hyperkalaemia
  - Hypotension
  - Cardiogenic shock
  - Other effects can include, hypo/hyperglycaemia, pulmonary oedema, seizures, coma

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why or how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm and mixed overdose
- Onset of toxicity may be delayed in modified-release preparations
- Propranolol and sotalol are particularly dangerous in overdose:
  - Propranolol may cause seizures, coma, and QRS widening
  - Sotalol may prolong QT and precipitate torsades de pointes
- Effects may be compounded when other cardiac or sedating drugs are co-ingested
- Excessive fluid loading may worsen pulmonary oedema, so these patients often require vasoactive support rather than repeated large fluid boluses

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if the patient is awake and cooperative
- Position the patient appropriately
- Perform repeated vital signs, including BGL
- Give oxygen if clinically indicated
- Consider activated charcoal only within local protocol and appropriate time window
- Have a low threshold for contacting the current poison permit holder, toxicology support, or RFDS

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Gain IV access in all significant cases
- Consider fluids only in cautious boluses if indicated, to avoid overload and pulmonary oedema
- Consider atropine in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider adrenaline in consultation with the on-call doctor
- In severe beta-blocker poisoning with hypotension or shock, early toxicology or medical consultation is essential
- Vasopressors and high-dose insulin therapy may be required in addition to standard supportive care
- Consider sodium bicarbonate if QRS widening is present, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider glucose if indicated
- Consider transcutaneous pacing, with appropriate sedation, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider magnesium sulphate for torsades de pointes in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider antiemetic therapy cautiously, noting QT issues in sotalol toxicity

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Clinical features typically manifest within 4 hours of ingestion
- Activated Charcoal: Mixed data on its efficacy; however, considered low harm intervention for potential benefits
- Atropine: Atropine alone is usually not enough for reversal in beta blocker overdose
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
- Vasoplegia, also known as Vasoplegic Syndrome, is a condition characterised by abnormally low systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and profound vasodilation leading to hypotension despite a normal or increased cardiac output

- Glucagon may be considered as an adjunct in consultation with a senior clinician or toxicology service, but response is variable and it should not be relied on as sole therapy
- It is unlikely that any site will carry enough Glucagon to follow this treatment through as a large volume is needed as an initial bolus dose, 5-10mg over 10 minutes and then an additional 1-5mg per hour infusion
